questions.text: Which cells secrete flexible, elastic, non-living matrix collagen that surrounds the cartilage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteocytes are mature bone cells.
Osteoclasts are bone-dissolving cells.

The cells that secrete the flexible, elastic, non-living matrix collagen that surrounds the cartilage are called chondroblasts or chondrocytes. These cells are specialized for cartilage formation and maintenance. Cartilage is a connective tissue found in various parts of the body, including the joints, ear, nose, and respiratory passages. Its unique properties make it an essential component in providing support, cushioning, and flexibility to the skeletal system.
Osteoblasts are bone-forming cells.

questions.explanation: 'Chondrocytes' are the first chondroblast cells that produce the collagen extracellular matrix (ECM) and then get caught in the matrix. Options A, B, and D are wrong because all three cells are related to bone, whereas option C is related to cartilage.

questions.text: From the following options, which one is non-cellular in most cases in animals?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chlorenchyma is a type of plant tissue that contains chloroplasts and is involved in photosynthesis. It is found in the leaves of plants and plays a crucial role in capturing light energy and converting it into chemical energy. Chlorenchyma is not found in animals; it is a plant-specific tissue.

Mesoderm is one of the three primary germ layers that form during embryonic development in animals. It gives rise to various structures, such as muscle, bone, connective tissue, and the circulatory system. Mesoderm is a cellular tissue and is essential for the proper development and organization of animal bodies.

Sclerenchyma is a type of plant tissue characterized by thick, lignified cell walls that provide strength and support to plant parts. It is commonly found in mature plant tissues like stems, roots, and seed coats. Sclerenchyma is not present in animals; it is specific to plants.

Mesenchyme is a type of connective tissue found in animal embryos, especially during early stages of development. It is a non-cellular, gelatinous matrix in which cells are suspended. Mesenchyme plays a crucial role in embryonic development, as it gives rise to various tissues, including bone, cartilage, and muscle.

questions.explanation: Chlorenchyma, Mesoderm, and Sclerenchyma are cellular, so the correct answer is D: Mesenchyme.

questions.text: Cartilage is a form of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cardiac muscle (or myocardium) makes up the thick middle layer of the heart. It is one of three types of muscle in the body, along with skeletal and smooth muscle.
Connective tissue supports, protects, and gives structure to other tissues and organs in the body, and this is what cartilage does.
The epithelial tissue is a type of body tissue that forms the covering on all internal and external surfaces of your body, lines body cavities and hollow organs, and is the major tissue in glands.
Nervous tissue is found in the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. It is responsible for coordinating and controlling many body activities.

questions.explanation: The correct option is B. Cartilage is a form of connective tissue. Cartilage is a strong, flexible connective tissue that protects your joints and bones. It acts as a shock absorber throughout your body. Cartilage at the end of your bones reduces friction and prevents them from rubbing together when you use your joints or perform movements.

questions.text: Cartilage has living cells that are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteocytes are cell that lies within the substance of fully formed bone.
Osteoclasts are large multinucleated cells responsible for the dissolution and absorption of bone.
Chondrocytes are cells of cartilage.
Osteoblasts are cells that form bone tissue.

questions.explanation: Cartilage cells are called chondrocytes. OPTION A: Osteocytes are cell that lies within the substance of fully formed bone. OPTION B: Osteoclasts are large multinucleated cells responsible for the dissolution and absorption of bone. OPTION C: Chondrocytes are cells of cartilage. OPTION D: Osteoblasts are cells that form bone tissue.

questions.text: Which type of cartilage is the most abundant in the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyaline cartilage is the most abundant type of cartilage in the human body. It is found in the nose, Trachea, and bronchi, Larynx, Costal cartilages (connecting ribs to sternum), Articular surfaces of bones, and Embryonic skeleton (before ossification).
Found only in a few places, like the external ear (pinna), epiglottis, and Eustachian tube. It provides flexibility, not abundance.
Found in intervertebral discs, pubic symphysis, and menisci of the knee. It’s strong but less common.
Incorrect, since hyaline cartilage is clearly the most abundant.

questions.explanation: Hyaline cartilage is the most common type of cartilage. Where bone ends meet to form a joint, they are covered by hyaline cartilage. This cartilage appears bluish white and glistening in a normal, healthy joint. Its primary function is to provide some cushioning and minimize friction between the bone ends.

questions.text: The fibrous connective tissue that attaches a muscle to a bone is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

Ligaments are also fibrous connective tissues, but they serve a different function. Ligaments connect bones to other bones at the joints and provide stability to the joints, preventing excessive movement and protecting them from injury.

Reticular tissue is a type of connective tissue found in various organs and tissues, such as the liver, spleen, and lymph nodes. It forms a supportive framework within these organs but does not attach muscles to bones.

Cartilage is a type of connective tissue that is softer and more flexible than bone. It is found in various parts of the body, such as the nose, ears, and joints. Cartilage does not attach muscles to bones; instead, it provides cushioning and support at the joints.

questions.explanation: Option (a) is correct. Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

questions.text: What is not true about cartilage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: There are many blood vessels in the cartilage
options.explanation: A is correct as Cartilage does not have a proper blood supply, unlike bone, which has blood vessels.

options.text: It is a form of connective tissue
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect because cartilage is a form of connective tissue. It plays a structural role, providing support and flexibility to various parts of the body, such as the joints, ears, and nose.

options.text: It covers ends of the bone at the joint
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because cartilage covers the ends of bones at the joint. It acts as a smooth and protective surface, reducing friction and facilitating movement between bones in the joint.
Only option A is correct.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct as "cartilage" is the main type of connective tissue found throughout the body in bones, joints, spine, lungs, and nose. It has structural and functional purposes and does not contain any blood vessels.

questions.text: The fibrous connective tissue which attaches bone to bone is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons attach muscles to bones, not bones to bones.
Ligaments are strong, fibrous tissues that connect bones to other bones.
Reticular tissue forms a network of fibers that support soft organs, not bone connections.
Cartilage is a flexible tissue that cushions joints, but does not connect bones directly.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Ligament. Ligaments are tough bands of fibrous connective tissue that connect one bone to another, providing stability to the joints. Tendons, on the other hand, connect muscle to bone and are not involved in bone-to-bone connections. Reticular tissue is not involved in the direct connection of bones; it provides a supportive framework for organs. Cartilage is a flexible tissue that covers the surfaces of joints for cushioning but does not serve as a connective link between bones.

questions.text: What protein, present in sarcomeres, is responsible for the passive elasticity of muscle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Collagen's main role is to provide structure, strength, and support throughout your body. Hence, this isn’t the correct answer.
In a muscle, myosin molecules are located in myofibrils, which make up the contractile unit of a muscle cell. Hence, this is incorrect.
Titin is a massive protein that spans the length of half of a sarcomere (from the Z-disc to the M-line) and allows for the passive elasticity of muscle.
Actin and Myosin are the force-generating proteins of the sarcomere, and they work together during the muscle contraction cycle to move. Hence, this isn’t correct.

questions.explanation: Titin is a massive protein that spans the length of half of a sarcomere (from the Z-disc to the M-line) and allows for the passive elasticity of muscle.

questions.text: What is the correct sequence of the bones in the arm of a mammal, from the hand to the shoulder?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Phalanges -> Carpal -> Humerus -> Radius and Ulna -> Scapula
options.explanation: This sequence incorrectly places the Humerus before the Radius and Ulna.

options.text: Phalanges -> Radius and Ulna -> Carpal -> Humerus -> Scapula
options.explanation: This sequence incorrectly places the Radius and Ulna before the Carpal bones.

options.text: Phalanges -> Carpal -> Radius and Ulna -> Humerus -> Scapula
options.explanation: This is the correct sequence of bones from the hand to the shoulder.

options.text: Carpal -> Phalanges -> Radius and Ulna -> Humerus -> Scapula
options.explanation: This sequence incorrectly places the Carpal bones before the Phalanges.

questions.explanation: In a mammalian arm, the correct sequence of bones from the hand to the shoulder is: Phalanges -> Carpal -> Radius and Ulna -> Humerus -> Scapula. The Phalanges are the bones of the fingers, followed by the Carpal bones forming the wrist. The Radius and Ulna are the two bones of the forearm, the Humerus is the upper arm bone, and the Scapula is the shoulder blade. Incorrect sequences either misplace the Carpal bones, the Humerus, or the Radius and Ulna.

questions.text: Coccyx vertebrae are located in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cervical spine is the most superior portion of the vertebral column, lying between the cranium and the thoracic vertebrae. It consists of seven distinct vertebrae.
The lumbar spine (lower back) consists of five vertebrae in the lower part of the spine, between the ribs and the pelvis. The five lumbar vertebrae are numbered L1 to L5.

The coccyx, also known as the tailbone, is a small, triangular bone resembling a shortened tail located at the bottom of the spine in the pelvic region. It is composed of three to five coccygeal vertebrae or spinal bones.

The thoracic spine is the longest region of the spine, and by some measures it is also the most complex. Connecting with the cervical spine above and the lumbar spine below, the thoracic spine runs from the base of the neck down to the abdomen. The thoracic spine has 12 vertebrae stacked on top of each other, labelled from T1 down to T12.

questions.text: Skull, vertebral column, ribs, and sternum form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The appendicular skeleton is the portion of the skeleton of vertebrates consisting of the bones that support the appendages. There are 126 bones. The appendicular skeleton includes the skeletal elements within the limbs, as well as the supporting shoulder girdle, pectoral, and pelvic girdle.
A hydrostatic skeleton is a skeleton formed by a fluid-filled compartment within the body, called the coelom.
An exoskeleton is an external skeleton that supports and protects an animal's body.
The axial skeleton forms a vertical axis that includes the head, neck, back, and chest. It has 80 bones and consists of the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage.

questions.explanation: The axial skeleton forms a vertical axis that includes the head, neck, back, and chest. It has 80 bones and consists of the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage. The appendicular skeleton is the portion of the skeleton of vertebrates consisting of the bones that support the appendages. There are 126 bones. The appendicular skeleton includes the skeletal elements within the limbs, as well as the supporting shoulder girdle, pectoral, and pelvic girdle. A hydrostatic skeleton is a skeleton formed by a fluid-filled compartment within the body, called the coelom. An exoskeleton is an external skeleton that supports and protects an animal's body.

questions.text: Scapula is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tailbone, scientifically known as the coccyx, is a small, triangular bone at the base of the spine. It consists of three to five fused vertebrae and is situated just above the cleft of the buttocks. The coccyx serves as an attachment point for various muscles, ligaments, and tendons. While it doesn't have a major role in weight-bearing or movement, it plays a part in supporting the pelvic floor and provides attachment points for muscles involved in posture and movement of the lower back and pelvis.

The hip bone is a large, flat bone forming part of the pelvis. It consists of three fused bones: the ilium, ischium, and pubis. Together, they create the socket for the hip joint and contribute to the structure of the pelvis, providing support for the body and attachment points for muscles.

The skull is the bony structure that encloses and protects the brain. It consists of two main parts: the cranium, which houses the brain, and the mandible (lower jaw), which is involved in chewing and speech. The skull also protects and supports sensory organs, such as the eyes and ears.

The scapula or shoulder blade is the bone that connects the clavicle to the humerus. The scapula forms the posterior of the shoulder girdle. It is a sturdy, flat, triangular bone. The scapula provides attachment to several groups of muscles.

questions.explanation: The scapula or shoulder blade is the bone that connects the clavicle to the humerus. The scapula forms the posterior of the shoulder girdle. It is a sturdy, flat, triangular bone. The scapula provides attachment to several groups of muscles.

questions.text: Clavicle is present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vertebral column is also called the spinal column or backbone. It's made up of 33 vertebrae and does not contain the clavicle.
The skull is a bony structure that supports the face and forms a protective cavity for the brain. It is comprised of many bones, but the clavicle is not one of them.
The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

A pectoral girdle, also referred to as the shoulder girdle, connects your upper limbs to the bones along the axis of your body. The pectoral girdle consists of the two bones that make up your shoulder: the clavicle, or collarbone. Scapula, or shoulder blade. The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

questions.text: The total number of cervical and thoracic vertebrae in human vertebral column is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The human vertebral column consists of 33 individual vertebrae, which are classified into different regions. The cervical region is located in the neck area and consists of 7 vertebrae while the thoracic region consists of 12 vertebrae.
The cervical region consists of 7 vertebrae, and the thoracic region consists of 12 vertebrae, so this is correct.
There is no specific region of the vertebral column that contains 14 vertebrae. The total number of vertebrae in the human vertebral column is 33.
It represents the total number of vertebrae.

questions.explanation: There are 7 vertebrae in the cervical region and 12 in the thoracic region, so 19 is the correct answer.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures holds the bones together:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Joints are where two or more bones meet, allowing for movement and flexibility. While they are crucial for mobility, they are not primarily responsible for holding bones together.
Cartilage is a resilient and smooth elastic tissue that covers and protects the ends of long bones at the joints, but it does not hold bones together.
Fibrous capsules are part of a joint structure, surrounding the joint to provide stability and contain synovial fluid, but they do not directly hold bones together.
Ligaments are strong bands of fibrous connective tissue that connect bones to one another and provide stability to joints. They are essential for holding bones together.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Ligaments. Ligaments are fibrous connective tissues that connect bones to other bones, providing joint stability and holding the skeletal structure together. Joints are places where bones meet to allow movement, but they do not connect bones. Cartilage acts as a cushion at the ends of bones to reduce friction and absorb shock but does not join bones together. Fibrous capsules are part of the joint structure, providing an enclosing sheath around the joint, but they do not hold the bones together like ligaments do.

questions.text: The first vertebrae of the cervical region of the vertebral column is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The first vertebra of the cervical region is known as the atlas. It supports the skull and allows for nodding movements of the head.
The sacral vertebrae are located in the sacrum, which is a fused bone at the base of the vertebral column.
The thoracic vertebrae are located in the thoracic region of the spine and are associated with the rib cage.
The second vertebra of the cervical region is known as the axis. It has a specialized structure called the odontoid process that allows rotational movement of the head.

questions.explanation: The first vertebra of the cervical region is known as the atlas. It supports the skull and allows for nodding movements of the head.

questions.text: The human and mammalian skeleton can be divided into two parts, the axial skeleton and:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The human and mammalian skeleton can be divided into the axial skeleton ( cranium, backbone, ribs) and the appendicular skeleton (upper and lower skeleton).
It is a rigid external covering in invertebrates, especially arthropods.
It is an internal skeleton present in vertebrates.
It is a water-filled cavity, usually present in invertebrates.

questions.explanation: The human skeleton consists of the axial skeleton, which comprises the cranium, backbone, and ribs, and the appendicular skeleton, which consists of the upper and lower skeleton.

questions.text: How many bones are involved in the formation of each half of the pelvic girdle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that three bones are involved in the formation of each half of the pelvic girdle. However, this is not correct. The pelvic girdle is composed of two halves, and each half is formed by the fusion of three individual bones: the ilium, ischium, and pubis.
This option suggests that four bones are involved in the formation of each half of the pelvic girdle. Again, this is not correct. The pelvic girdle is formed by the fusion of three bones, not four.

This option suggests that two bones are involved in the formation of each half of the pelvic girdle. This is also not correct. As mentioned earlier, each half of the pelvic girdle is formed by the fusion of three individual bones.

This option suggests that one bone is involved in the formation of each half of the pelvic girdle. This is not correct. The pelvic girdle is a complex structure formed by the fusion of three separate bones on each side of the body.

questions.explanation: Each half of the pelvic girdle comprises 3 bones: the ilium, the pubis, and the ischium.

questions.text: Which one of the following bones is the only movable portion of the skull?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The maxilla is the stationary upper bone of the jaw, fixed in place and does not move independently.
The frontal bone forms the forehead and part of the eye sockets. It is part of the fixed portion of the skull.
The mandible, or lower jaw, is the only movable bone of the skull, allowing the mouth to open and close.
The zygomatic bone, or cheekbone, is part of the fixed structure of the skull and does not move.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the mandible. It is the only bone in the skull that moves, allowing for the opening and closing of the mouth, which is essential for chewing and speaking. The other options, such as the maxilla, frontal bone, and zygomatic bone, are part of the fixed structure of the skull and do not have independent movement.

The axial skeleton forms a vertical axis that includes the head, neck, back, and chest. It has 80 bones and consists of the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage.

questions.explanation: The axial skeleton forms a vertical axis that includes the head, neck, back, and chest. It has 80 bones and consists of the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage. The appendicular skeleton is the portion of the skeleton of vertebrates consisting of the bones that support the appendages. There are 126 bones. The appendicular skeleton includes the skeletal elements within the limbs, as well as the supporting shoulder girdle, pectoral, and pelvic girdle. A hydrostatic skeleton is a skeleton formed by a fluid-filled compartment within the body, called the coelom. An exoskeleton is an external skeleton that supports and protects an animal's body.

questions.text: Which one of the following cartilages is the most abundant in the human body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Elastic cartilage is a structural cartilaginous tissue for non-load-bearing body parts, such as the ears, nose, and epiglottis.

Chondral cartilage is a thin, smooth material that covers the ends of the thigh bone and shinbone (the femur and tibia). It reduces friction as the bones glide against each other during movement of the knee.
Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons.

A translucent bluish-white type of cartilage present in the joints, the respiratory tract, and the immature skeleton is known as hyaline cartilage. It is the most abundant cartilage in the human body.

questions.explanation: A translucent bluish-white type of cartilage present in the joints, the respiratory tract, and the immature skeleton is known as hyaline cartilage. It is the most abundant cartilage in the human body.

questions.text: The name of the second vertebrae in the neck region is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The atlas bone is the top bone or first cervical vertebra connecting your skull to your spine. It's also known as the C1 vertebra in your cervical spine.

The cervical spine (neck region) consists of seven bones (C1-C7 vertebrae), which are separated from one another by intervertebral discs. Your first vertebra (C1), also called the atlas, is a ring-shaped bone that begins at the base of your skull. It's named as an atlas. The second cervical vertebra, or axis, supports the odontoid process.

Your neck vertebra, also known as the cervical spine, consists of the first seven vertebrae in your spine. It provides support for the weight of your head, surrounds and protects your spinal cord, and allows for a wide range of head motions.
A rib is said to be "floating" if it does not attach to the sternum. It is one of the last 2 ribs and is not found in the beginning.

questions.explanation: The cervical spine (neck region) consists of seven bones (C1-C7 vertebrae), which are separated from one another by intervertebral discs. Your first vertebra (C1), also called the atlas, is a ring-shaped bone that begins at the base of your skull. It's named the atlas. The second cervical vertebra, or axis, supports the odontoid process.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the correct matching of three items and their grouping category?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Ilium, ischium, and pubis → Coxal bones of the pelvic girdle
options.explanation: The ilium, ischium, and pubis are three bones that come together to form the coxal bone, part of the pelvic girdle. This grouping is correct as these bones provide support and stability to the pelvis, crucial for weight-bearing and movement.

Actin and myosin are indeed muscle proteins crucial for muscle contraction. However, rhodopsin is not a muscle protein but a visual pigment involved in the phototransduction process in the retina. This makes the grouping incorrect.
Cytosine and uracil are pyrimidine bases but guanine is purine base found in nucleic acids making this grouping incorrect.
The malleus, incus, and stapes comprise the ear ossicles. The cochlea is a separate structure in the inner ear responsible for converting sound into nerve impulses. Thus, this grouping is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Ilium, ischium, and pubis → Coxal bones of the pelvic girdle. These bones fuse to form the coxal bone, part of the pelvic girdle, which is crucial for supporting body weight and facilitating movement by connecting the spine to the lower limbs. Option B is incorrect because, although actin and myosin are muscle proteins, rhodopsin is a visual pigment, not related to muscles. Option C is incorrect as cytosine and uracil are pyrimidines, but guanine is a purine base, not a pyrimidine. Option D is incorrect because the cochlea is not one of the ear ossicles; the correct third ossicle is the stapes.

questions.text: Bone related with skull is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The coracoid is part of the shoulder girdle in the scapula, unrelated to the skull.
The arytenoid cartilages belong to the larynx, not part of the skull bones.
The pterygoid processes are part of the sphenoid bone, contributing to the skull's structure.
The atlas is the first cervical vertebra, supporting the skull but not part of it.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Pterygoid. The pterygoid processes are extensions of the sphenoid bone, integral to the skull's structure and located near its base. The other options are incorrect: The Coracoid is in the shoulder girdle, the Arytenoid is in the larynx, and the Atlas, although it supports the skull, is a cervical vertebra and not part of the skull itself.

questions.text: The number of floating ribs in the human body is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The human body has 2 pairs of floating ribs. The eleventh and twelfth ribs are the ones classified as floating.
Correct. The human body has 2 pairs of floating ribs, specifically the eleventh and twelfth ribs, which do not connect to the sternum.
Incorrect. There are only 2 pairs of floating ribs. The rest are categorized as true or false ribs.
Incorrect. The correct count is 2 pairs of floating ribs, which are the last two pairs in the rib cage.

questions.explanation: In the human body, there are 2 pairs of floating ribs, which are the eleventh and twelfth ribs. These ribs are unique because they do not attach to the sternum or the cartilage of other ribs, unlike true ribs (first seven pairs) and false ribs (eighth to tenth pairs). The other options incorrectly suggest a higher number of floating ribs, which are not supported by anatomical structure.

questions.text: A deltoid ridge occurs in?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The radius does not have a deltoid ridge; it is primarily involved in the rotation of the forearm.
The ulna does not have a deltoid ridge; it is mainly known for forming the elbow joint with the humerus.
The femur, while being a major bone in the leg, does not feature a deltoid ridge, which is specific to the humerus.
The humerus is the correct answer as it features a deltoid ridge, which is a crucial site for muscle attachment, including the deltoid muscle.

questions.explanation: The deltoid ridge is a prominent anatomical feature found on the humerus, a bone in the upper arm. This ridge serves as a critical attachment point for the deltoid muscle, which is responsible for lifting the arm. The radius and ulna are bones in the forearm and do not have a deltoid ridge, while the femur, a bone in the leg, is unrelated to the deltoid muscle. Thus, the humerus is the only bone among the options that features a deltoid ridge.

questions.text: Ribs which are only attached to spinal cord are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: True ribs are the first seven pairs of ribs that directly attach to the sternum, so they are not only attached to the spinal cord.
False ribs are the lower five pairs of ribs that attach indirectly to the sternum through cartilage or not at all, differing from floating ribs.
Floating ribs are the last two pairs of ribs that are only attached to the vertebrae and not to the sternum, making them unique in their lack of anterior attachment.
The costal arch is formed by the cartilages of the false ribs, creating a curved structure at the lower part of the ribcage, not a separate set of ribs.

questions.explanation: Floating ribs are the correct answer because they are the last two pairs of ribs in the human ribcage that do not connect to the sternum at all, only attaching to the vertebrae at the back. This distinguishes them from true ribs, which attach directly to the sternum, and false ribs, which have indirect or no sternum attachment via the costal cartilage. The costal arch is unrelated as it is a structure formed by the costal cartilages of the false ribs.

questions.text: Protein present in cartilage is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Collagen is a structural protein found in various connective tissues, including skin and bone, but it is not the primary protein in cartilage.
Chondrin is a key component of cartilage, providing it with the necessary flexibility and resilience, making it the correct answer.
Myosin is a motor protein primarily involved in muscle contraction and is not associated with cartilage structure.
Elastin is a protein that provides elasticity to tissues but is not a major component of cartilage.

questions.explanation: The matrix of cartilage contains chondrin, a protein-carbohydrate complex that gives it flexibility.

questions.text: Ligament is a/an:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ligaments are not inelastic or white; they are elastic and yellow in color.
Ligaments are not white in color; they are yellow and contain elastic fibers.
Correct! Ligaments occur in the form of cords in a modified yellow elastic fibrous tissue and connect bone with bone, providing stability to joints.
Incorrect. One of the options correctly describes ligaments as modified yellow elastic fibrous tissue.

questions.explanation: Ligaments are composed of modified yellow elastic fibrous tissue and connect bone to bone, providing stability and support to joints. Option A and B are incorrect as ligaments are not inelastic or white in color. Option D is incorrect as one option correctly describes ligaments.

questions.text: Three of the following pairs of the human skeletal parts are correctly matched with their respective inclusive skeletal category and one pair is not matched. Identify the non matching pair.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a correct match. The sternum and ribs are integral components of the axial skeleton, which includes the skull, vertebral column, and rib cage, forming the central structure of the human skeleton.

options.text: Clavicle and glenoid cavity - Pelvic girdle
options.explanation: This is not a correct match. The clavicle and the glenoid cavity are part of the pectoral girdle in the appendicular skeleton, not the pelvic girdle. The pelvic girdle consists of the hip bones, which facilitate the attachment of the lower limbs.
This is a correct match. The humerus and ulna are bones of the upper limb, fully part of the appendicular skeleton, which includes the limbs and their girdles.
This is a correct match. The malleus and stapes are tiny bones located in the middle ear, known as ear ossicles, crucial for the auditory process.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Clavicle and glenoid cavity - Pelvic girdle. This pair is incorrectly matched because both the clavicle and glenoid cavity belong to the pectoral girdle in the appendicular skeleton, not the pelvic girdle. The pelvic girdle consists of the hip bones, which are essential for supporting the weight of the upper body when standing and for locomotion.Option A is correct because the sternum and ribs belong to the axial skeleton, forming the protective rib cage. Option C is correct as the humerus and ulna are part of the appendicular skeleton, specifically the upper limb. Option D is correct since the malleus and stapes are ear ossicles, functioning in the auditory system.

questions.text: In the human body, which one of the following is anatomically correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Humans have 1 pair of collar bones, also known as clavicles.
Incorrect. There are 3 pairs of major salivary glands: parotid, submandibular, and sublingual glands.
Incorrect. There are 12 pairs of cranial nerves in the human body.
Correct. The human rib cage includes 2 pairs of floating ribs, which are the 11th and 12th pairs of ribs, not attached to the sternum.

questions.explanation: In the human body, there are normally 12 pairs of ribs. Among these, the floating ribs (11th and 12th pairs) do not connect to the sternum, distinguishing them anatomically from the other ribs. This makes Option D correct. In contrast, Option A incorrectly states the number of collar bones, Option B incorrectly lists the number of salivary gland pairs, and Option C underestimates the number of cranial nerve pairs, making them all incorrect.

questions.text: Three of the following pairs of the human skeletal parts are correctly matched with their respective inclusive skeletal category and one pair is not matched. Identify the nonmatching pair.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sternum and ribs are part of the axial skeleton, which includes bones along the body's central axis.

options.text: Clavicle and glenoid cavity - Pelvic girdle
options.explanation: The clavicle and glenoid cavity are part of the pectoral girdle, not the pelvic girdle, which includes the hip bones.
The humerus and ulna are part of the appendicular skeleton, which includes the limbs and girdles that attach them to the axial skeleton.
The malleus and stapes are indeed part of the ear ossicles, which are small bones located in the middle ear.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Clavicle and glenoid cavity - Pelvic girdle. The clavicle and glenoid cavity are actually components of the pectoral girdle, not the pelvic girdle. The pelvic girdle includes the hip bones, not the clavicle or glenoid cavity.Option A is incorrect because the sternum and ribs are accurately categorized under the axial skeleton. Option C is incorrect as the humerus and ulna do belong to the appendicular skeleton. Option D is correct in stating that the malleus and stapes are part of the ear ossicles.

questions.text: Vertebrae of the neck are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The coccyx (plural: coccyges) is the series of rudimentary vertebrae forming the caudal termination of the vertebral column and is positioned inferior to the apex of the sacrum. The vertebral column is composed of seven cervical, twelve thoracic, five lumbar, five sacral, and four coccygeal vertebrae.

The cervical spine is the most superior portion of the vertebral column, lying between the cranium and the thoracic vertebrae. It is also called the cervical vertebrae. There are 7 cervical vertebrae in the human body.
The sacrum, sometimes called the sacral vertebra or sacral spine (S1), is a large, flat, triangular-shaped bone nested between the hip bones and positioned below the last lumbar vertebra.
The lumbar vertebrae are the five vertebrae between the rib cage and the pelvis. They are the largest segments of the vertebral column.

questions.explanation: In tetrapods, 'cervical vertebrae' (singular: vertebra) are the vertebrae of the neck, immediately below the skull. Truncal vertebrae (divided into thoracic and lumbar vertebrae in mammals) lie caudal (toward the tail) of cervical vertebrae.

questions.text: In a human vertebral column, the number of _ vertebrae is 7.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There are 7 cervical vertebrae in the human neck.
12 Thoracic vertebrae are found in the human body.
There are five bones in the lower back, i.e, the lumbar region.
There are five sacral vertebrae, which are fused.

questions.explanation: There are 7 cervical vertebrae in the human neck.

questions.text: Which one of the following cartilages is the most abundant in the human body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Elastic cartilage is a structural cartilaginous tissue for non-load-bearing body parts, such as the ears, nose, and epiglottis.

Chondral cartilage is a thin, smooth material that covers the ends of the thigh bone and shinbone (the femur and tibia). It reduces friction as the bones glide against each other during movement of the knee.
Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons.

A translucent bluish-white type of cartilage present in the joints, the respiratory tract, and the immature skeleton is known as hyaline cartilage. It is the most abundant cartilage in the human body.

questions.explanation: A translucent bluish-white type of cartilage present in the joints, the respiratory tract, and the immature skeleton is known as hyaline cartilage. It is the most abundant cartilage in the human body.

questions.text: Clavicle is present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vertebral column is also called the spinal column or backbone. It's made up of 33 vertebrae and does not contain the clavicle.
The skull is a bony structure that supports the face and forms a protective cavity for the brain. It is comprised of many bones, but the clavicle is not one of them.
The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

A pectoral girdle, also referred to as the shoulder girdle, connects your upper limbs to the bones along the axis of your body. The pectoral girdle consists of the two bones that make up your shoulder: the clavicle, or collarbone. Scapula, or shoulder blade. The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

questions.explanation: A pectoral girdle, also referred to as the shoulder girdle, connects your upper limbs to the bones along the axis of your body. The pectoral girdle consists of the two bones that make up your shoulder: the clavicle, or collarbone. scapula, or shoulder blade. The pelvic girdle, or the bony pelvis, is a bony ring, formed by the left and right hip bones and the sacrum, and it surrounds the pelvic cavity and connects the vertebral column to the lower limbs.

questions.text: The sternum is connected to ribs by?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While areolar tissue is a type of loose connective tissue, it does not serve as the connecting medium between the sternum and ribs, which requires a more suitable cartilage for support and flexibility.
This is incorrect. Bony matter refers to bone tissue itself, which does not provide the necessary flexibility needed for the connection between the sternum and the ribs.
This is the correct answer. Hyaline cartilage is the type of cartilage that connects the ribs to the sternum, allowing for the flexibility and movement needed during activities such as breathing.
This option is incorrect. White fibrous cartilage is dense and not suitable for the flexible connection needed between the sternum and ribs, which is why hyaline cartilage is used instead.

questions.explanation: Costal cartilages are hyaline cartilage, providing flexibility during breathing.

questions.text: Which one of the following are the correct pairing of a body part and the kind of muscle tissue that moves it?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Biceps of the upper arm – Smooth muscle fibres
options.explanation: The biceps of the upper arm are actually made up of skeletal muscles, not smooth muscles.
The abdominal wall primarily consists of skeletal muscles not smooth muscle which make this option incorrect.
Correct. The iris of the eyes is made up of involuntary smooth muscle tissue, which is controlled by the autonomic nervous system.
The heart wall actually consists of cardiac muscles, which are striated, not unstriated.

questions.explanation: Smooth muscles are called involuntary muscles because the action of these muscles is controlled by the autonomic nervous system, meaning they are not under conscious control. The iris of the eyes contains smooth involuntary muscles, making option C the correct answer. The biceps of the upper arm are skeletal muscles, not smooth muscles as mentioned in option A. Option B is incorrect as the abdominal wall does contain skeletal muscles not smooth muscles. Option D is incorrect since the heart wall is primarily composed of cardiac muscles, not unstriated muscles.
